item.title: Emacs Org-Roam: Gestione della Conoscenza a Grafi e Metodo Zettelkasten
item.description:
youtube

Abbandonare la rigida struttura gerarchica a cartelle e sottocartelle è il primo passo per costruire un sistema di gestione della conoscenza personale (PKM) davvero efficiente. In questo video esploriamo come trasformare Emacs in un potente "secondo cervello" non gerarchico attraverso Org-Roam, un'estensione ispirata ai principi metodologici dello Zettelkasten. Analizzeremo la teoria dei grafi applicata alle tue note, esaminando come l'estensione traduca i concetti di nodi e collegamenti bidirezionali (backlinks) in metadati nativi di Org-mode, senza ricorrere a formati proprietari ma sfruttando la persistenza degli ID alfanumerici registrati su database SQLite. -- Contatti -- Canale telegram: https://t.me/NFVblog Pagina ko-fi per le donazioni: https://ko-fi.com/nfvblog Pagina Mastodon: https://mastodon.cloud/@nfvblog

item.title: Xah Talk Show Ep793. emacs, install Xah Fly Keys in WSL linux emacs
item.description:
youtube

Video Summary (Generated by AI, Edited by Human.) In this video, Xah Lee demonstrates the process of installing his custom Emacs configuration, specifically Xah Fly Keys, within a Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) environment. The session is highly technical and covers his troubleshooting process for setting up a functional development environment in Linux. Key Highlights: • Goal and Motivation: The primary objective is to migrate his established Emacs workflow from Windows to WSL, ensuring he can use his custom keybindings and packages effectively (0:17-1:24). • Technical Challenges: A significant portion of the video details the hurdles encountered, such as: • Font Configuration: Debugging why font sizes and families (specifically Deja Vu Sans Mono) were not applying correctly in the Linux environment (51:38, 57:26, 1:04:19). • File Management: Navigating the Linux filesystem and managing init files compared to his usual Windows setup (1:30:03-1:49:45). • Performance: Comparing the startup speed of Emacs on Windows versus WSL (1:55:07-1:56:15). • Philosophy and Commentary: Xah Lee provides commentary throughout on his distaste for certain Unix conventions, particularly the historic 80-character line-length limitation, which he argues causes unnecessary formatting issues for modern users (2:07:31-2:12:35). Conclusion: By the end of the video, Xah Lee successfully got Xah Fly Keys set his Emacs setup on WSL. also mentioned is that there seems to be missing native code compilation on the WSL instance. (2:16:49-2:17:58). Notes at http://xahlee.info/talk_show/xah_talk_show_ep793.html If browser says security error, that is because it auto changes URL from HTTP to HTTPS. It's a way to censor 22 million old websites. Remove the S in HTTPS. Or use brave browser or safari.

item.title: Vibe Coding with Emacs' Built-In AI - System Crafters Live!
item.description:
youtube

In this stream, we'll finally give vibe coding a try! But forget the big-name cloud assistants: it turns out Emacs has been shipping with its own AI assistant for decades, so we'll be pair programming with that instead. The rules are simple: we start a fresh project, we consult our assistant before every decision, and whatever it says, goes. Let's see if we can learn what all the vibe coding fuss is about! ,#emacs #vibecoding #aicoding #pairprogramming #lisp SUPPORT THE CHANNEL: 👍 Support My Work: https://systemcrafters.net/how-to-help/#support-my-work 📰 Subscribe to the Newsletter: https://systemcrafters.net/newsletter/ 👕 Check out the Store: https://systemcrafters.store 📘 Get Your Copy of Mastering Emacs: https://www.masteringemacs.org/r/systemcrafters?utm_source=yt&utm_medium=desc&utm_campaign=scme SHOW NOTES: https://systemcrafters.net/live-streams/june-12-2026/ JOIN THE COMMUNITY: https://systemcrafters.net/community/ (Forum and IRC chat!) https://fosstodon.org/@daviwil MY CONFIGURATION: (This site is currently down but will be back up soon!) https://config.daviwil.com https://config.daviwil.com/emacs https://config.daviwil.com/systems (Guix) OTHER SERIES: - Emacs Essentials: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=48JlgiBpw_I&list=PLEoMzSkcN8oPZvSdewHG8uApD7THlLLCV - Emacs From Scratch: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=74zOY-vgkyw&list=PLEoMzSkcN8oPH1au7H6B7bBJ4ZO7BXjSZ - Emacs Tips: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wKTKmE1wLyw&list=PLEoMzSkcN8oMHJ6Xil1YdnYtlWd5hHZql - Emacs Desktop Environment: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=f7xB2fFk1tQ&list=PLEoMzSkcN8oNPbEMYEtswOVTvq7CVddCS - Emacs IDE: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E-NAM9U5JYE&list=PLEoMzSkcN8oNvsrtk_iZSb94krGRofFjN - Emacs Mail: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yZRyEhi4y44&list=PLEoMzSkcN8oM-kA19xOQc8s0gr0PpFGJQ - Learning Emacs Lisp: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RQK_DaaX34Q&list=PLEoMzSkcN8oPQtn7FQEF3D7sroZbXuPZ7 - Craft Your System with GNU Guix: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iBaqOK75cho&list=PLEoMzSkcN8oNxnj7jm5V2ZcGc52002pQU CREDITS: Coriolis Effect by logos feat. stefsax, licensed Creative Commons 3.0 CC-BY http://ccmixter.org/files/mseq/26296 reNovation by airtone, licensed Creative Commons 3.0 CC-BY http://ccmixter.org/files/airtone/60674 ukeSounds by airtone, licensed Creative Commons 3.0 CC-BY http://ccmixter.org/files/airtone/32655 Between Worlds (Instrumental) by Aussens@iter, licensed Creative Commons 3.0 CC-BY http://ccmixter.org/files/tobias_weber/56664

item.title: Yay Emacs 33: Sacha and Prot Talk Emacs: Built-ins
item.description:
youtube

https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/06/june-11-sacha-and-prot-talk-emacs-built-ins/ On June 11, I'm going to chat with Prot https://protesilaos.com about the Emacs Carnival June 2026 topic Underappreciated Emacs Built-ins https://rossabaker.com/blog/underappreciated-emacs-built-ins/. Thanks to Ross A. Baker for hosting the carnival! 2026-06-11 Thu 10:30--2026-06-11 Thu 11:30 (America/Toronto) = Thu Jun 11 1030H EDT / 0930H CDT / 0830H MDT / 0730H PDT / 1430H UTC / 1630H CEST / 1730H EEST / 2000H IST / 2230H +08 / 2330H JST

item.title: Xah Talk Show Ep792. install emacs on wsl Windows Subsystem for Linux
item.description:
youtube

In this episode of Xah Talk Show, the creator walks viewers through the process of installing and compiling Emacs on the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL). The session is informal, characterized by the creator's commentary on technology, his views on software development history, and his personal anecdotes. Key sections of the video include: Introduction to WSL: The creator explains how to enable the necessary virtualization settings in the BIOS/UEFI and the basic commands to initialize WSL on Windows (0:00 - 9:00). The Installation Process: Throughout the video, the creator struggles with and successfully navigates installing dependencies (such as build-essential, libxpm, libgtk, etc.) required to build Emacs from source (13:30 - 1:18:20). Historical Commentary: The creator offers his perspectives on the history of package managers (e.g., Think, MacPorts, Homebrew, dpkg), the evolution of open-source software, and the influence of corporations like Microsoft and Google (51:45 - 55:40). The Ubuntu Logo Story: A notable tangent where the creator discusses the origin and perceived ideological implications of the Ubuntu logo (36:34 - 41:43). Successful Compilation and Demo: After a long build process, the creator successfully runs the Emacs binary (version 32) within WSL and performs a quick demo of the Tetris game built into Emacs to verify the installation (1:44:00 - 1:46:39). Notes at http://xahlee.info/talk_show/xah_talk_show_ep792.html If browser says security error, that is because it auto changes URL from HTTP to HTTPS. It's a way to censor 22 million old websites. Remove the S in HTTPS. Or use brave browser or safari.

item.title: Emacs - world-cup.el: Gettin' hyped for the 2026 World Cup
item.description:
youtube

I pretty much only get into soccer for the world cup every four years -- at which point, I have a lot of studying to do! I'd way rather do that in emacs than anywhere else. So I vibecoded world-cup.el -- and here's me showing it off. Source Repo: https://github.com/strickinato/org-world-cup-2026/ Project that inspired: https://www.sastibe.de/2018/06/its-here-org-agenda-for-the-world-cup-2018/ Don't forget, touch grass...

item.title: How to Install a Package on Emacs From Melpa
item.description:
youtube

In this video, I show you how to install a custom package from the Melpa package repository by first enabling packages at startup and then how, without elisp, to add the Melpa package repository. I then show you how to install the theme "Atom One Dark" and also a cool website to use to find themes.

item.title: How I structure my Emacs init.el file
item.description:
youtube

I cover how my init.el file is structured. You can find it on my github: https://github.com/maxfriis/my-emacs-config Note that I forgot to mention, why I don't use use-package or straight. The reason is that I don't want to micromanage what get deferred. I trust that the packages I use defer sensibly. Furthermore if you want to utilize use-package, I feel it invites you to have a more messy structure. E.g. I like that all my keybindings are in a separate section.

item.title: How to Change Themes in Emacs
item.description:
youtube

In this video, I show you how to change from the default theme of Emacs into another one of the themes that comes with Emacs by using the command: M-x customize-themes I then show you how to change to that theme and save that theme preference for future sessions when using Emacs.

item.title: Corrigindo divergências entre arquivos de configuração do emacs
item.description:
youtube

Me veja tentando resolver divergências acumuladas nos últimos 6 meses.

item.title: How to Inhibit the Welcome Screen in Emacs
item.description:
youtube

In this video, I show you how to inhibit the welcome screen in Emacs so that you can start the editor with a blank buffer. To do this, I have you customize the option (with M-x customize-option) "inhibit-startup-screen". You'll want to toggle that to on and then click "apply and save" within the editor. After that, when you restart the editor it will start with a blank buffer.

item.title: How to Maximize Emacs' Screen Upon Startup
item.description:
youtube

In this video, I show you how to maximize the Emacs editor upon startup with the following commands: M-x "customize-option" This first command allows you to customize a variable. The variable you want to customize is "default-frame-alist". With this variable you want to create a parameter called "fullscreen" and give it the value "maximized". You'll then click apply and save and the next time you startup Emacs it will maximize to the full screen size.

item.title: June 4: Emacs Chat with Ben Zanin (@gnomon@mastodon.social)
item.description:
youtube

https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/06/june-4-emacs-chat-with-ben-zanin-gnomon-mastodon-social/ I chatted with Ben Zanin about music, Org Mode, Emacs in terminals and on an Android, keyboards, elfeed, and more. - Ben Zanin (@gnomon@mastodon.social) - Mastodon https://mastodon.social/@gnomon: Robertson screwdriver owner, believer in the value of personal-scale computing and skeptic of the value of computing scales any larger than that - ~gnomon/emacs-config-gnomon - An edited/truncated subset of my dotfiles repo to pick out only those commits and files that are pertinent to my emacs configuration, as of 2026-05-06 - sourcehut git https://git.sr.ht/~gnomon/emacs-config-gnomon - ~gnomon's git repositories https://git.sr.ht/~gnomon/ - https://melpa.org/#/elfeed, https://melpa.org/#/elfeed-curate - https://melpa.org/#/versuri and https://melpa.org/#/mpdel - lyrics, music - https://melpa.org/#/avy - WezTerm - Wez's Terminal Emulator https://wezterm.org/index.html - Keychron B11 Pro Ultra-Slim Wireless Foldable Keyboard (US ANSI Layout – Keychron Canada https://keychron.ca/products/keychron-b11-pro-ultra-slim-wireless-foldable-keyboard-us-ansi-layout - tea-timer and https://elpa.gnu.org/packages/tmr.html 00:00 How Ben got started with Emacs 02:19 Ben got into Emacs because of twittering-mode 04:30 Emacs as a media playback platform with Versuri and Mpdel 09:13 Emacs on Android with Termux 13:44 Keyboards 16:44 Benefits of a split keyboard 18:22 Meeting workflow 21:11 Narrowing 22:58 There's even an internal Slack channel about Emacs at Ben's company 24:47 Ben keeps Org capture templates as individual files and adapts them to different meeting flows 28:45 Personal-scale software and the journey 34:07 vc-git-grep for finding notes again 37:47 Keybindings and terminals; wezterm 42:38 Timers: tea-timer, tmr 43:57 Different stages of package use 44:47 Elfeed 53:13 Bookmark naming conventions 53:50 elfeed-curate for annotations 55:46 mytoots archives Mastodon toots 57:05 Mentoring offer 58:02 A local instance of public-inbox can let you use Gnus to read mailing lists quickly You can add the iCal for upcoming Emacs Chat episodes to your calendar. https://sachachua.com/topic/upcoming-emacs-chats.ics Find more Emacs Chats or join the fun: https://sachachua.com/emacs-chat

item.title: How to Hide the Tool Bar, Scroll Bar, and Menu Bar in Emacs
item.description:
youtube

In this video, I show you how to hide the tool bar, scroll bar, and menu bar in Emacs without writing any elisp. All you have to do is M-x "customize-option" then customize the following variables: - tool-bar-mode - scroll-bar-mode - menu-bar-mode You can change these, click "Apply and Save" and have Emacs write your configuration to your .emacs file so that it automatically starts up with your preferred settings. I also show you how to bring back the menu-bar and talk about how you can do the same thing with the scroll-bar and the tool-bar if you so choose with some simple editor commands.

item.title: Kokoro-onnx with Cuda for text to speech with Emacs
item.description:
youtube

Kokoro-onnx for text to speech with Cuda on Freebsd with Emacs Using a simple python script to create an api using fastapi and uvicorn we can use kokoro-onnx using curl on the command line or using emacs and a couple of lines of elisp code Cuda support on Freebsd using podman Note you should be able to get this working on Linux, Mac and Windows as well Supports multiple languages Offer multiple voices Lightweight: ~300MB (quantized: ~80MB) kokoro-onnx github https://github.com/thewh1teagle/kokoro-onnx freebsd-cuda project https://github.com/NapoleonWils0n/freebsd-cuda kokoro-onnx podman container for freebsd https://github.com/NapoleonWils0n/freebsd-cuda#kokoro kokoro-onnx install with cuda uv init -p 3.12 uv venv uv pip install kokoro-onnx soundfile "onnxruntime-gpu[cuda,cudnn]" fastapi you will also need the following packages installed libsndfile libffi-devel download the model files https://github.com/thewh1teagle/kokoro-onnx/releases/download/model-files-v1.0/kokoro-v1.0.onnx https://github.com/thewh1teagle/kokoro-onnx/releases/download/model-files-v1.0/kokoro-v1.0.onnx kokoro-api script https://github.com/NapoleonWils0n/freebsd-cuda/blob/master/containers/python/kokoro-onnx/bin/kokoro-api note you if you arent using the podman continer you will need to edit the script and change the MODEL_PATH and VOICES_PATH paths https://github.com/NapoleonWils0n/freebsd-cuda/blob/master/containers/python/kokoro-onnx/bin/kokoro-api#L39 @sachactube text to speech https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/03/small-steps-towards-using-openai-compatible-text-to-speech-services-with-speechd-el-or-emacspeak/ freebsd root https://github.com/NapoleonWils0n/freebsd-root-xps freebsd dotfiles https://github.com/NapoleonWils0n/freebsd-dotfiles-xps freebsd bin https://github.com/NapoleonWils0n/freebsd-bin-xps

item.title: CEDET for Plan 9 coding
item.description:
youtube

Plan 9 C is not ANSI C. So existing tools don't help. Details: https://lifeofpenguin.blogspot.com/2021/04/gnu-emacs-as-lightweight-ide.html

item.title: How to Setup Emacs on a Chromebook Without Elisp in 2026
item.description:
youtube

In this video, I show you how to setup Emacs on a Chromebook in 2026 without elisp, just using built in commands within the Emacs environment that write to your .emacs file. This video covers how to maximize the Emacs screen upon loading, how to load Emacs without the get started screen, how to change the default font of Emacs to a custom font, how to get rid of the tool bar, the scroll bar, and the menu bar as well as how to change the theme of Emacs after showing you how to install packages from Melpa including the Doom themes package. Additionally, I show you how to change the status bar of Emacs to the doom modeline package, noting that you must install nerd icon fonts to get the icons to display properly.

item.title: This perfect text editor doesn't exist
item.description:
youtube

I have a list of mandatory requirement for The Perfect Text Editor, that I have in mind for a long time. Here it is. Yes, I am very opinionated. Terminal editors are bad: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tvlupFXjqpQ Fred Wookash ep: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XexxO5noRVM 4coder Wookash ep: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oA9nTuO62VI Chapters: 00:00 - Intro 00:34 - Should be graphical 02:20 - Variable width fonts 03:10 - Physical scan codes 05:00 - Smooth 4k performance 06:40 - Cross-platform 06:50 - Modal editing 07:20 - Arbitrary programmable 09:07 - Real extension programming language 09:50 - Editing of big files 11:08 - Multiple cursors 11: 40 - Self-documenting 13:36 - Existing art 15:15 - Outro

item.title: Beyond ICR: Incremental 'Suggesting' Read in Emacs!
item.description:
youtube

This video hypothesizes what lies beyond the envelope of Incremental Completing Read, a notion I've coined "Incremental Suggesting Read". Read the blog post or watch the video for more information: https://www.chiply.dev/post-incremental-suggesting-read Links: - John Kitchin's original implementation of semantic completing read : https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bB7rywlahSc&t=293s - karthink's gptel: https://github.com/karthink/gptel - Armin Darvish's consult-omni: https://github.com/armindarvish/consult-omni Timestamps: 0:00 Intro 3:10 Semantic Retrieval 3:38 Generative Synthesis 4:18 Demo of Semantic Retrieval 9:40 Demo of Generative Synthesis Part 1 15:10 Demo of Generative Synthesis Part 2 18:30 Outro ,#emacs #ai #semanticsearch #software #programming

item.title: самый хайповый гайд на Emacs
item.description: youtube

item.title: How to Install Emacs on a Chromebook in 2026
item.description:
youtube

In this video, I show you how to install Emacs on a Chromebook in 2026. The first step I go through is how to enable the Linux development environment with Crostini through your settings. After that, I show you how to update the Linux development environment with the commands: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y Once the Linux development environment is updated I show you how to install Emacs with the command: sudo apt install emacs And how, if you don't add the -y, you will be prompted if you want to use the extra space. Finally, I show you how to pin the Emacs Gui to your taskbar so you can easily open Emacs everyday you use it.

item.title: I Replaced Obsidian with Doom Emacs & Org Mode
item.description:
youtube

I think I found the one! Emacs Doom has me thinking about it non-stop. I still haven't figured out half of what it can do. But I'm eager to find out! The animations in the beginning were done with motion canvas, just writing this here so I can call you out for not reading the description. Chapters: 0:00 Intro 1:26 Installing Doom Emacs on NixOS 3:28 Getting started 7:00 Learning Emacs 11:04 General Emacs Commands 13:13 Final thoughts & Outro ,#NixOS #Neovim #Neorg #Linux #Notetaking #Opensource #emacs #emacsdoom

item.title: Emacs Chat 24: Emacs Chat with Omar Antolin Camarena
item.description:
youtube

https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/05/may-29-emacs-chat-with-omar-antolin-camarena/ I chatted with Omar Antolín Camarena about Emacs, keyboard macros, temporary buffers, Embark, and other workflow tips. 00:00 Ignore this part 00:18 Opening 00:46 How did you get into Emacs in the first place? 06:01 Repeating edits 07:28 dot-mode: repeating commands 09:24 block-undo: undo things as a chunk 10:29 Starting and stopping keyboard macros 12:15 Keycast and Embark 13:33 apply-macro-to-lines-of-paragraph 16:34 embark-on-last-message 18:06 tmp-buffer with a major mode 19:26 placeholder 20:38 enable-recursive-minibuffers 22:57 Overriding embark-select 23:32 quick-calc 26:28 Multiple cursors 27:40 Block-undo and regular undo 28:53 Cycling through Embark targets 31:40 Imenu for navigation 32:51 Collaboration 38:00 Technology adoption and Emacs packages 40:04 Personal packages and naming conventions 42:26 find-file-at-point and directory names 43:46 The value of using Emacs’s APIs 44:54 org-ql and usual files 47:04 Shortcuts for org-ql search syntax 47:41 Org TODO states: TODO, WAIT, DONE, NOPE 48:25 The inserter macro 50:05 luggage: generative art experiments 53:49 Teaching and Emacs 54:53 The print10 generator 56:23 arXiv 58:29 Toggle keymap 01:00:54 isearch-delete-wrong 01:03:14 isearch - continue from the beginning of the match 01:05:12 Using keymaps to remember sets of commands 01:06:04 Other things from the config Related links: - Omar Antolín Camarena https://www.matem.unam.mx/~omar/: a researcher at Instituto de Matemáticas, UNAM in Mexico City - M-x apropos Emacs https://www.matem.unam.mx/~omar/apropos-emacs.html - oantolin/emacs-config: My personal Emacs configuration · GitHub https://github.com/oantolin/emacs-config - oantolin/placeholder: Emacs package to treat any buffer as a template with placeholders to fill-in · GitHub https://github.com/oantolin/placeholder - oantolin (Omar Antolín Camarena) · GitHub https://github.com/oantolin - Omar Antolín (@oantolin@mathstodon.xyz) - Mathstodon https://mathstodon.xyz/@oantolin - u/oantolin on Reddit https://www.reddit.com/user/oantolin/ - #Emacs #Embark with Omar Antolin, Ramin Honary and Kent Pitman #lisp and more #interview #lispyGopherClimate - toobnix https://toobnix.org/w/752ix2RNx5BijosuYtSGCv This session will be recorded, and I'll update this blog post with notes: https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/05/may-29-emacs-chat-with-omar-antolin-camarena/ You can add the iCal for upcoming Emacs Chat episodes to your calendar. https://sachachua.com/topic/emacs-chat/upcoming-emacs-chats.ics Find more Emacs Chats or join the fun: https://sachachua.com/emacs-chat

item.title: Xah Talk Show Ep787. emacs lisp. add width height to all thumbnails
item.description:
youtube

Video Summary (Generated by AI, Edited by Human.) • In this video, Xah Lee discusses his technical plan to retroactively add width and height attributes to image tags across his personal website, which contains approximately 12,000 HTML pages (32:20). • He explains that he had previously removed these attributes to keep his source code clean, but now realizes they are necessary to prevent Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)—a common web development issue where page content unexpectedly shifts during loading (34:30). Key concepts covered: • Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS): The author explains how omitting image dimensions causes browsers to re-render the layout as images load, negatively impacting the user experience (34:30 - 38:00). • Emacs Lisp Coding: The video features the author navigating his codebase using his custom Xah Fly Keys system. • He demonstrates various Emacs commands, including how to manipulate HTML tags and extract urls (7:45, 19:15, 23:35). • Efficiency and Workflows: The author reflects on his preference for using his own tools, such as his custom HTML mode, rather than relying on third-party solutions (19:25). • Potential Solutions: He outlines two primary technical approaches for the migration: either processing files to replace existing tags with updated ones using custom Lisp functions or building a database (hash table) of image dimensions to programmatically inject attributes into existing tags (57:05 - 59:20). • Throughout the video, the author also digresses into topics like the history of various keyboard layouts (Dvorak, Colemak, etc.), the benefits of his specific key-binding system, and some personal reflections (8:05 - 12:50, 15:20).

item.title: Yay Emacs 32: Sacha and Prot Talk Emacs: May I recommend...
item.description:
youtube

Notes: https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/05/may-28-sacha-and-prot-talk-emacs-may-i-recommend/ In this livestream, I chatted with Prot https://protesilaos.com about the May 2026 Emacs Carnival theme "May I recommend https://www.emacswiki.org/emacs/CarnivalMay2026". I'd love to turn this into a joint braindump of quick recommendations for people at different points in their Emacs journey, building on our conversation about newbies/starter kits https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/04/yay-emacs-sacha-and-prot-talk-emacs-newbies-starter-kits/ and the newcomer experience https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/05/may-14-sacha-prot-and-philip-kaludercic-talk-emacs-newcomer-experience/ all the way up to power users, Emacs Lisp coders, and package developers. 00:00 Opening 02:27 Tip: Less is more. Start small. 04:07 Tip: Start with what is built in 04:27 Skill: Figuring out the words to look for 06:25 Tip: Be okay with starting over 08:16 Skill: Learning to discover 08:42 Tip: Read manuals for fun 10:16 Tip: Use Emacs bookmarks to save your place in the manual 10:43 Tip: Generally, investing time into navigation and note-taking workflows pays off 12:19 Skill: Keyboard macros 12:53 Skill: Modifying the behavior of code via hooks and advice 13:15 Tip: Learn to think in terms of buffers and windows 14:07 Skill: Reading the source code; Tip: Just jump in 15:33 Tip: edebug is great for exploring code 16:26 Tip: Reading tests can help you understand code, too. 17:02 Skill: Idiomatic Elisp 17:17 Tip: Write tests. 17:52 Tip: When writing Emacs Lisp that expects a list, use plurals 18:59 Tip: When naming, be verbose rather than terse 19:46 Tip: Iterate on your workflow in small steps 20:20 Tip: Make things more automatic, and use context-sensitive clues 24:48 Skill: Thinking in terms of elements 26:16 Skill: Reading other people's configuration and adapting ideas to yours 27:07 Tip: Start with focusing on just one thing 27:33 Blog posts and videos are useful 28:09 Tip: Take notes as you learn, and ideally, share them too. 28:54 Tip: Accept being a beginner. 31:16 Group: Power users 32:13 Tip: Browse through package lists 32:25 Tip: Dive deeply into the packages you have: customization options, code, etc. 32:41 Tip: find-library gets you to the source code, occur can help you browse it 33:29 Tip: You can also browse through Customize 33:48 Tip: Have fun with randomness and serendipity 34:32 Tip: Check out people's workflow descriptions and stories 35:36 Resources: manuals, Mastering Emacs, Emacs Lisp Elements 37:29 Skill: Figuring out what's possible and making a habit of writing tiny functions 37:45 Skill: Being mindful of what you do over and over again 38:26 Tip: Keyboard macros can help you jumpstart custom functions 39:11 Tip: Use C-h k (describe-key) to describe shortcuts or menu items 40:04 You can set up M-x to show keyboard shortcuts too (Marginalia?) 41:26 Resource: Emacs from Scratch series by System Crafters 41:50 Tip: Old tutorials can still be useful, although don't treat them as the sole source of truth (things may have changed since then) 42:55 Skill: Finding preferred resources 44:37 Tip: If you find your tribe, look for ways to keep in touch with them 44:58 Tip: Manage unequal RSS frequencies with folders or tags 46:33 Tip: Doing more things in Emacs has compounding benefits 48:31 Tip: Learn to think of it as just text 49:46 Tip: Take notes along the way 50:16 Tip: Explore different ways to navigate and act on things 51:09 Tip: Learn to combine different building blocks 52:47 Tip: Get the hang of keybinding conventions 56:06 Tip: Use which-key for keybinding help 57:41 Tip: Figure out your ergonomics You can add the iCal for upcoming Yay Emacs episodes to your calendar. https://sachachua.com/topic/live/upcoming-livestreams.ics Find more Yay Emacs posts or join the fun: https://sachachua.com/topic/live

item.title: Shibuya.lisp lispmeetup #118 2026-05-28
item.description:
youtube

2026-05-28開催のShibuya.lisp #118の様子です イベントページ: https://lisp.connpass.com/event/391947/ conao3さん Emacsを作っている話 niyarinさん 壊れたカッコを直すプログラム カマイルカさん "Simple Made Easy" Made Easier: Clojureに学ぶsimplicity

item.title: space-tree: Workspace Management Trees in Emacs!
item.description:
youtube

space-tree is a workspace management tool for Emacs. It's key differentiator among the plethora of workspace tools in Emacs is its ability to organize workspaces in a hierarchy, or tree like structure. See blog post here: https://www.chiply.dev/post-emacs-space-tree 0:00 Intro 2:40 Workspace 1 4:10 Workspace 2 5:50 Workspace switching 6:40 Workspace 3 8:40 Workspace 4: Complex 12:00 Workspace 5: Branched 15:40 Switching sub-spaces 17:00 Bliss point branch depth 17:50 All space-tree commands 20:15 Naming spaces 21:50 Switching spaces by name 22:50 Pruning spaces 24:40 Other space packages 27:20 Outro ,#emacs #workspace #humancomputerinteraction #software #programming #textediting

item.title: Faça o Emacs olhar para o dinheiro usando o Embark
item.description:
youtube

Nesse breve vídeo, eu mostro como você pode fazer com que o emacs saiba o que fazer com o dinheiro usando o embark, especificamente no contexto do hledger-mode.

item.title: You can do what with org agenda?
item.description:
youtube

The default org-agenda shows you a basic 7-day span, fine for the week ahead useless you've got deadlines scattered across the next month and want to see them in one view without warning days. Turns out two settings unlock a different picture to unstick your schedule. Yes. Let's do this. Let's walk through: - why org-agenda hides your long-range deadlines by default - what org-deadline-warning-days actually does - how to extend the agenda span to 20 days - a custom command you can pull up with a single keystroke, without overriding your global warnings CHECK OUT MY BOOKS: https://chrismaiorana.com/books/

item.title: From GNU Emacs to code gouv fr
item.description:
youtube

Continue watching these same videos also found outside of YouTube: www.gnu.org/audio-video/audio-video.html media.libreplanet.org/videos By fsf LibrePlanet 2022 videos This talk is titled "From GNU Emacs to code.gouv.fr," and was presented at LibrePlanet 2022 by Bastien Guerry. Bastien is the free software officer for the French public administration. With a background in philosophy and cognitive sciences, he became interested in the free software movement in the late 90's and taught himself how to program by contributing to GNU Emacs. He has been involved with, among other things, One Laptop Per Child and Wikimedia France. This talk is about Bastien's own history and motivations as a free software hacktivist, and what progressively led him to dedicate more time and energy to support free software in the public sector, as well as provide a few practical tips on how everyone may do so in everyday life. Licence CC Attribution - Share Alike www.framatube.org/w/tu4rw4YiWimZvLdAD6iEWH

item.title: May 21: Emacs Chat with Raymond Zeitler
item.description:
youtube

https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/05/emacs-chat-with-raymond-zeitler/ I chatted with Raymond Zeitler about Emacs, life, automation, Org Mode, Diary, and Calendar. There were a couple of cuts to get rid of accidentally shown passwords, but it was a great glimpse into someone's system for managing things. 00:00 Opening 00:58 Introduction 01:59 I love automating workflows 03:15 Org Mode switch 04:08 diary-float 06:48 Tip: Add links to task titles 06:59 diary-float 07:59 The difference between active timestamps and SCHEDULED 10:06 Including other diary files 11:26 cal-tex-cursor-week-iso, printing planner pages on index cards 14:59 Holidays 16:45 Making calendars for other people 17:27 Keeping track of when things were done on the house 18:49 My first customizations: backspace, buffers 20:32 Windows and super key 23:23 Org Mode class on Udemy, agenda custom commands 25:00 toggling tags 26:57 TODO states 27:21 Functions for Org Agenda 28:34 exeln, shellfn: executing things in DOS 30:22 Middle mouse click 31:59 Keybindings in other apps: Vivaldi 34:09 M-s M-w, eww-search-words 35:50 Saving links with org-store-link 38:29 How I got into Emacs 41:45 Maybe my own theme? 42:26 Other editors? Always Emacs 43:57 Package names 45:54 What's next? Maybe auto maintenance 48:31 Vibe-coding? 50:53 Where people can find me 52:02 Org Mode source blocks 52:49 Slideshows? 53:50 Emacs Chats? 56:33 Other resources that would be nice to have Find more Emacs Chats or join the fun: https://sachachua.com/emacs-chat

item.title: Annotate-in-Place Notes with Emacs and org-remark!
item.description:
youtube

This video demonstrates org-remark's implementation of the annotate-in-place pattern in Emacs. I also talk broadly about the benefits of this kind of note taking pattern, and how it has both improved my note taking experience as well as my reading experience. 0:00 Intro 2:30 Annotate File Demo 6:30 Annotate Webpage Demo 8:45 Other Use Cases 10:05 Annotate Elfeed Demo 11:50 Daily Annotation Review ,#emacs #notes #knowledge #notetaking #developertools #coding

item.title: Apropos with Bozhidar Batsov
item.description:
youtube

Welcome to Apropos! This episode features special guest Bozhidar Batsov, creator of CIDER.

item.title: Doom Emacs Mail Sending and Python Way
item.description:
youtube

Simple mail sending Using Doom Emacs Config and Python Way. Useful for automating tasks , reports , Notifications etc

item.title: What's So Special About Emacs?
item.description:
youtube

A quick tour of some Emacs features for those who have never used it. 0:00 Intro 0:23 Functions 0:59 Keybindings 1:40 Editing Python 2:34 Editing C++ 3:13 Muscle Memory 3:32 Email 3:59 RSS 4:26 Claude 4:54 File Manager 5:06 Git & Terminal 5:25 Packages 6:18 Org Mode 7:44 In Summary

item.title: YE29: Sacha, Prot, and Philip Kaludercic Talk Emacs: Newcomer Experience
item.description:
youtube

https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/05/may-14-sacha-prot-and-philip-kaludercic-talk-emacs-newcomer-experience/ Philip Kaludercic https://social.sdfeu.org/@pkal/116500474344904460 wanted to continue the conversation from YE24: Sacha and Prot Talk Emacs - Newbies/Starter Kits https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/04/yay-emacs-sacha-and-prot-talk-emacs-newbies-starter-kits/. He's spent a lot of time thinking about this as one of the main contributors to newcomers-presets, so there'll probably be much to cover! 00:00 Opening 02:59 newcomers-presets user option theme; would be nice to explain what the changes are 05:00 finding a balance between "it's fine the way it is" and "just use Doom Emacs" 06:37 people value stability, but also conventions have shifted. 06:50 ways Emacs does things differently: ex: terminal vs eshell, output is editable; new users want to edit the previous prompt; sometimes goes against people's intuitions 09:21 How do people develop Emacs intuition? Immersion 09:55 example: dabbrev, there's no undo? Ah, it's just the regular undo. 11:00 newcomers presets: smooth over the intuition-disrupting things that are not actually necessary/beneficial; ex: enable whichkey 14:32 newcomers-presets choice is not saved at the moment 17:08 newcomers without much computing experience might even find it easier (no C-c expectations, C-v etc) 18:30 Focus group? 22:15 Emacs survey before 22:48 people's backgrounds influence their responses 23:46 Hypothetical: Reset themes, to reset things back to the defaults of a specific Emacs version 24:22 package-autosuggest-mode suggests based on file extension 27:52 Emacs 32: bundled versions of Emacs (Big Emacs - distributions that include more packages) 29:54 Selection versus multiple completion 34:39 Manuals 35:08 More examples? 36:21 find-user-init-file? 38:38 Getting over the reverence for Emacs's history 40:11 Changes are more likely to happen when someone puts in the work to make a patch 44:03 Preserving Git history of packages absorbed into the core 46:00 Dealing with multiple types of Emacs 48:09 Fat Emacs is just about bundling more packages from ELPA, not changing the configuration for them 51:23 Customize 54:41 CUA - Common User Access 55:00 ini file format? https://sdf.org/~pkal//blog/emacs/ini-init.html 55:10 Emacs configuration generator 55:54 INI-style configuration 01:00:21 Quick summary 01:02:27 Continuing with INI 01:04:42 Motivation 01:06:50 Politics and philosophy 01:09:23 Experimenting with things outside core 01:10:42 Extending the core 01:11:52 Guide to contributing to ELPA 01:13:11 Making the newcomer experience better 01:14:30 "user option themes" versus "appearance themes" 01:16:49 configuration generator in Emacs? maybe more wizards? 01:16:59 Starter kits 01:17:39 Configuration generator in Emacs Lisp? 01:18:40 extending the archive format 01:20:56 User interfaces Related links: - A proposal for a "beginners" (user-option) theme - Philip Kaludercic https://yhetil.org/emacs-devel/87wm0f2rng.fsf@posteo.net/ - Re: some file-related options to consider for newcomers-presets - Philip Kaludercic https://yhetil.org/emacs-devel/87tsuucbkh.fsf@posteo.net/ - A newcomer's feedback on newcomer presets - Abdulnafe Toulaimat https://yhetil.org/emacs-devel/CABPi7cHxEoMKwvDkuXGZXaHDpUuqS6ry60JOcdSZbSuYB47LSg@mail.gmail.com/

item.title: Exam minimal Emacs, 42KL, try not to buy Armageddon Psychsparrow, what am I doing.
item.description:
youtube

Exam minimal Emacs, 42KL, try not to buy Armageddon Psychsparrow, what am I doing. Actually, I like that keyboard, but I want a split and Im not going to spend another penny for at least a few months. Might as well make the most out of this silly keyboard. Its not bad actually, if only you could just drop in a stiffer spring, Im sure someone has done it. Supporting me will inspire me to produce more quality content Show some support by buying me coffee on my Ko-fi page. It accepts your usual Debit/Credit card with 0% fee. https://ko-fi.com/ardiemejia83 Or can donate straight to my PayPal https://paypal.me/ardiemejia83

item.title: How to automatically scale emacs to a larger font on startup (on Raspberry Pi)
item.description:
youtube

Walkthrough of the contents of a .emacs file that scales up the default font size when starting emacs on a 1920x1200 Raspberry Pi screen.

item.title: Emacs Buffer Comparison Made Easy With Ebuffers 2026 05 10 02:31:35
item.description:
youtube

In this video, you will see how buffers should be compared and seen inside Emacs. ,#linux #linuxadmin #sysadmin #opensource #Gentoo #Debian #Slackware #research ,#linuxkernel #kernel #gnu #Linux #GNU Buy Me A Coffee: https://www.buymeacoffee.com/unixbhaskar Patreon: https://www.patreon.com/unixbhaskar Paypal: https://paypal.me/linuxbhaskar You can also find me : GitHub: https://github.com/unixbhaskar Reddit: https://www.reddit.com/user/unixbhaskar Blogs: https://unixbhaskar.wordpress.com Twitter: https://twitter.com/unixbhaskar Linkedin: https://linkedin.com/in/unixbhaskar Vimeo: https://vimeo.com/unixbhaskar Goodreads: https://goodreads.com/unixbhaskar
